message:
fix(pthread): TSD destructor not invoked on thread exit (bd-9hq64)

## Root cause

`pthread_threading_force_native_for_tests()` only set the thread-local
`FORCE_NATIVE_THREADING_OVERRIDE`. The pthread integration tests gate
it through `FORCE_NATIVE_ONCE: std::sync::Once::call_once(...)`, which
runs the setter on exactly one cargo-test worker thread (whichever
tripped it first). Every OTHER worker thread saw the override as
`None` and fell back to the global `FORCE_NATIVE_THREADING` atomic —
which was documented as "Set by pthread_threading_force_native_for_tests"
but NEVER actually stored true, so it stayed at its `AtomicBool::new(false)`
default.

Consequence: on every worker except the one tripping the `Once`,
`force_native_threading_enabled()` returned false, so `pthread_create`
took the `if !force_native && let Some(host_create) = …` branch and
dispatched to host libc's pthread_create via
`dispatch_host_thread_create_with_managed_attr`. The child thread
therefore:

- was NOT created through `native_pthread_create` / our clone trampoline,
- did NOT hit `super::tls::register_thread_tls` at trampoline line 228,
- did NOT hit `exit_current_thread` → `teardown_thread_tls` at exit.

`pthread_setspecific`'s `write_tls_value` saw `table_lookup(tid)` return
null (because `register_thread_tls` was never called) and fell through
to the `FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES` thread-local `Cell` array. Reads via
`pthread_getspecific` worked on the same thread (why
`ctx.observed_initial == 0` and `retval == ctx.value` asserted OK).
But at child exit, host libc's thread-exit machinery has no reason to
call OUR `teardown_thread_tls`, so the registered destructor never fired.

That's the exact bd-9hq64 fingerprint: setspecific/getspecific work,
thread-exit destructor silently skipped.

## Fix

crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/pthread_abi.rs: set both the global atomic
AND the thread-local override in
`pthread_threading_force_native_for_tests()`. First worker to trip the
`Once` now flips the global for every worker, every test, every
pthread_create.

## Defensive hardening

crates/frankenlibc-core/src/pthread/tls.rs: `teardown_thread_tls` now
falls through to `FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES` when the TID has no table entry,
so any future code path that stores specifics on a non-registered
thread still gets its destructor invoked at thread exit.
`FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES` is thread-local, so this is safe — only the
exiting thread can reach its own cells. This doesn't fix bd-9hq64 on
its own (the host-libc thread-exit path bypasses our teardown entirely
before the fallback walk could ever run) but it's the right semantic
for the core TSD module and catches the related failure mode where a
thread somehow skipped `register_thread_tls` but still hits
`teardown_thread_tls` through an explicit caller.

## Validation

- cargo test -p frankenlibc-abi --test pthread_tsd_test
  -> 14 passed, 0 failed (previously 13 passed, 1 failed on
  tsd_destructor_runs_on_thread_exit).
- cargo test -p frankenlibc-core --lib pthread
  -> 152 passed, 0 failed (no regression in the TSD backend).
- cargo test -p frankenlibc-abi --test pthread_abi_test
  -> 180 passed, 0 failed (full pthread ABI surface — no breakage
  from the global-atomic store).

Closes bd-9hq64.

Agent: cc-libc
Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

Requirements:
- Deterministic.
- O(1) update per observation.
- Fixed-point arithmetic (avoid floats on hot path).

Deliverables:
- alpha_investing.rs with controller state + summary.
- Unit tests for invariants (wealth bounds, spending rules).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:32:08.222982761Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T17:25:31.479124427Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T17:25:31.479098900Z","close_reason":"Alpha-Investing controller fully implemented in alpha_investing.rs (468 lines, 10 tests, wealth tracking, onset detection, cadence-gated). Formally blocked by bd-gn9 but implementation already follows the milli-units convention. Ready to close once bd-gn9 resolves.","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:20:50Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-9dq5","title":"Track D: Implement gai_cancel/gai_error/gai_suspend as synchronous-only stubs with proper error returns","description":"## Goal
Replace 3 async DNS stub functions in unistd_abi.rs (lines 17404-17423) that incorrectly return EAI_SYSTEM/ENOSYS with correct POSIX error returns.

## Critical Design Insight
FrankenLibC does NOT export getaddrinfo_a() — the function that creates async DNS requests. Therefore, gai_error/gai_cancel/gai_suspend will NEVER receive valid in-flight request handles. The correct implementation is NOT a thread pool — it is simply returning the correct error code for 'no such request' or 'no pending requests'.

## Current State (WRONG)
All 3 functions set errno=ENOSYS and return EAI_SYSTEM. This is incorrect because:
- gai_cancel should return EAI_NOCANCEL (not a valid request) or EAI_ALLDONE
- gai_error should return EAI_SYSTEM with errno=EINVAL (invalid gaicb)
- gai_suspend with an empty/null list should return EAI_ALLDONE (no pending requests)

Programs that probe async DNS availability by calling gai_suspend(NULL, 0, NULL) get EAI_SYSTEM instead of EAI_ALLDONE, which may cause them to disable async features unnecessarily.

## Correct Implementation (SIMPLE)
### gai_cancel(req)
- If req is NULL: return EAI_NOCANCEL
- Otherwise: return EAI_ALLDONE (no async requests exist, so it is already 'done')

### gai_error(req)
- If req is NULL: set errno=EINVAL, return EAI_SYSTEM
- Otherwise: return 0 (treat any gaicb as 'completed' since we resolve synchronously)

### gai_suspend(list, nitems, timeout)
- If list is NULL or nitems <= 0: return 0 (nothing to wait for)
- Scan list entries: if all non-NULL entries have ar_result set, return 0
- If timeout is NULL: return EAI_AGAIN (would block forever waiting for nothing)
- If timeout is not NULL: return EAI_AGAIN (timeout immediately since no async work pending)

## Why This Is Correct
The POSIX spec says gai_suspend waits for 'at least one request to complete.' Since FrankenLibC resolves all getaddrinfo calls synchronously, any gaicb passed to gai_suspend has ALREADY been resolved by the time gai_suspend is called. The correct behavior is to check if the requests are done and return immediately.

## Testing
- Unit test: gai_cancel(NULL) returns EAI_NOCANCEL
- Unit test: gai_cancel(non_null_ptr) returns EAI_ALLDONE
- Unit test: gai_error(NULL) returns EAI_SYSTEM with errno=EINVAL
- Unit test: gai_suspend(NULL, 0, NULL) returns 0
- Unit test: gai_suspend(empty_list, 0, NULL) returns 0
- e2e: scripts/check_gai_async_stubs.sh verifying correct return codes via small C test

## Structured Logging
All 3 functions must emit structured trace on invocation:
  trace_id, mode, api_family=resolv, symbol=gai_cancel|gai_error|gai_suspend, decision_path, errno, latency_ns

## Acceptance Criteria
All 3 functions return correct POSIX error codes. No ENOSYS returns. Tests pass. Clippy clean. e2e script passes.

## Note
If getaddrinfo_a() is ever implemented in the future, these functions would need to be upgraded to poll a real request registry. But that is a separate epic (P4+) since async DNS is extremely rare.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-03T00:19:24.489961531Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-03T02:46:07.802361147Z","closed_at":"2026-04-03T02:46:07.802082449Z","close_reason":"Replaced incorrect ENOSYS returns with correct POSIX error codes. gai_cancel: NULL->EAI_NOTCANCELED, non-null->EAI_ALLDONE (all sync, nothing to cancel). gai_error: NULL->EAI_SYSTEM/EINVAL, non-null->0 (synchronous resolver means complete). gai_suspend: nitems<=0->0, otherwise->0 (all requests already resolved synchronously). No thread pool needed since getaddrinfo_a is not exported. Clippy clean.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["async","resolv","stub-elimination"],"comments":[{"id":1364,"issue_id":"bd-9dq5","author":"ubuntu","text":"Risk: gai_* async DNS used by almost no programs. ENOSYS acceptable for 99%+ workloads. If too complex, fallback: implement gai_error as immediate-return (EAI_ALLDONE for completed, EAI_NOCANCEL for unknown) and gai_suspend as no-wait poll. This synchronous stub passes most programs checking async DNS availability.","created_at":"2026-04-03T00:21:02Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-9fbm","title":"ssignal_and_gsignal_deliver_signal test fails (SIGNAL_HIT is 0)","description":"The `ssignal_and_gsignal_deliver_signal` test in `unistd_abi_test.rs` fails because the signal handler installed by `ssignal` is not observing `SIGUSR1` when raised via `gsignal`. The `gsignal` call returns 0 (success), but `SIGNAL_HIT.load()` remains 0 instead of 10. This indicates a bug in the signal trampoline, `sysv_signal`'s translation, or signal delivery latency/blocking.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-18T19:12:28.460499626Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-18T19:21:05.797164282Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T19:21:05.795210404Z","close_reason":"No repro; signal same-thread delivery already fixed via tgkill path","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"bd-9hq64","title":"[HIGH] pthread_key_create destructor not invoked on thread exit (deterministic)","description":"## Symptom

cargo test -p frankenlibc-abi --test pthread_tsd_test has a deterministic failure:

```
test tsd_destructor_runs_on_thread_exit ... FAILED
---- tsd_destructor_runs_on_thread_exit stdout ----
thread 'tsd_destructor_runs_on_thread_exit' panicked at crates/frankenlibc-abi/tests/pthread_tsd_test.rs:331:
assertion `left == right` failed: thread exit should run the registered TSD destructor
  left: 0
 right: 17476

test result: FAILED. 13 passed; 1 failed
```

17476 = 0x4444, the context value the child thread stored via pthread_setspecific.

## Test flow (pthread_tsd_test.rs:295)

1. pthread_key_create(&mut key, Some(record_tsd_destructor)) → rc 0.
2. Child thread set ctx.value (0x4444) via pthread_setspecific(key, ptr).
3. Child thread exits by returning from tsd_roundtrip_start (i.e. normal thread exit — does not pthread_exit(3p) explicitly).
4. Main thread pthread_joins the child; retval matches ctx.value (this assertion passes, line 329).
5. Main thread reads TSD_DESTRUCTOR_LAST; expects it to contain 0x4444 because the registered destructor record_tsd_destructor() should have been called during child-thread teardown and stored the value it was handed. Actual value: 0. Destructor never ran.

## Diagnosis

POSIX and glibc specify that pthread_key_create-registered destructors are invoked during thread exit for every (key, value) pair whose value is non-NULL — **in up to PTHREAD_DESTRUCTOR_ITERATIONS passes** to drain re-registered keys. Our teardown path is not calling the registered destructor at all.

Candidate causes:

1. The TSD destructor table isn't being iterated during native-thread exit. Either the native-thread exit path doesn't walk the pthread_key_create-registered destructor list, or the list is wiped before the walk.
2. The child thread's TSD cell for this key is NULL at exit time. (pthread_setspecific may have failed silently? But the prior assertion at line 329 shows ctx.value round-trips through retval, which uses the same storage.)
3. The destructor is stored per key globally but keyed on a per-thread index that doesn't match what pthread_setspecific wrote.

## Fix direction

Read crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/pthread_abi.rs for pthread_key_create and the native-thread exit code path. Verify that:

- pthread_key_create stores the destructor function pointer per key.
- The native thread's exit routine walks every registered key, reads the thread's TSD slot for it, and, if non-NULL, sets the slot to NULL and calls the destructor with the old value.
- The walk runs before the thread's TLS storage is freed.

The authoritative reference is pthreads(7) and POSIX.1-2017 §2.9.2 'Thread-Specific Data'.

## Blast radius

Deterministic single test failure. Stale TSD from prior threads could leak memory in library users that rely on pthread_key_create destructors (e.g., every sensible C runtime's locale / errno / resolv-context TLS). This is also what a581f0ac / e289f838 were trying to get right on the __cxa_thread_atexit_impl side; the pthread_key TSD side appears to be a separate path that wasn't touched.

## Provenance

Found by cc-libc FULL REVIEW MODE at HEAD d485e53d br-bd-6kwnr: accept host condattr clocks. No SIGABRT, no parallel-race dependency — reproduces on isolated cargo test run of this single test.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T19:50:17.061480885Z","created_by":"cc-libc","updated_at":"2026-04-22T21:16:37.564274+00:00","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["abi","destructor","deterministic","full-review","pthread","test","tsd"],"notes":"## Bisection by cc-libc (2026-04-22 review-mode pass)

The core TSD logic works correctly in isolation — the unit test
`pthread::tls::tests::destructor_called_on_teardown`
(crates/frankenlibc-core/src/pthread/tls.rs:738) passes cleanly when run
directly: `cargo test -p frankenlibc-core --lib pthread::tls::tests::destructor_called_on_teardown`
→ 1 passed, 0 failed.

That unit test exercises: pthread_key_create(key, dtor) → register_thread_tls(tid, ptr) →
pthread_setspecific(key, val) → teardown_thread_tls(tid) → destructor invoked. When driven
directly against the core/src/pthread/tls.rs API, every step works. So the bug is NOT in:
 - KEY_REGISTRY_RCU registration / destructor storage (works in unit test).
 - teardown_thread_tls walk (works in unit test).
 - The seq + in_use check (works in unit test).
 - FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES vs table path (works in unit test).

That narrows the bug to the ABI / clone-trampoline integration path:

 - `pthread_abi::pthread_key_create` (pthread_abi.rs:2792) wraps core_pthread_key_create via
   with_threading_policy_guard. Both fallback and work closures call the core fn. Not
   ruled out but looks benign.
 - `pthread_abi::pthread_setspecific` (pthread_abi.rs:2868) same pattern, also calls
   core_pthread_setspecific in both closures.
 - `core/src/pthread/thread.rs` trampoline at line 170-241: calls register_thread_tls at
   line 228 with handle.tid.load(Ordering::Acquire), and exit_current_thread (line 241) →
   teardown_thread_tls(sys_gettid()) at line 319.
 - CLONE_PARENT_SETTID + CLONE_CHILD_CLEARTID interaction at thread.rs:546-548 — handle.tid
   holds the real TID until the child exits (CLONE_CHILD_CLEARTID only fires on exit).

## Strongest hypothesis

handle.tid at register_thread_tls time (line 228 — BEFORE start_routine runs) is
`handle.tid.load(Acquire)`. The parent's CLONE_PARENT_SETTID wrote the child's real TID
there, and the comment at thread.rs:564-568 says 'CLONE_PARENT_SETTID already published
tid into handle.tid' and warns that parent should NOT write tid again because
CLONE_CHILD_CLEARTID can clear it back to 0. So when the child's trampoline reaches line
228 it reads handle.tid which is the child's real TID.

But the code at thread.rs:190-194 shows the child's trampoline ALSO runs:

```
    let current_tid = syscall::sys_gettid();
    handle.self_tid.store(current_tid, Ordering::Release);
    if handle.tid.load(Ordering::Acquire) == 0 {
        handle.tid.store(current_tid, Ordering::Release);
    }
```

If we're somehow in the branch where `handle.tid.load() == 0` (maybe clone race on
short-lived child?), this stores the real TID. If NOT, handle.tid has the parent's view.
Either way, at line 228 handle.tid should equal sys_gettid().

EXCEPT: if CLONE_CHILD_CLEARTID fires before register_thread_tls (unlikely — we're still
pre-exit), OR if the child's sys_gettid() returns a different TID than CLONE_PARENT_SETTID
wrote (would need kernel bug).

## Suggested fix direction

Add an eprintln! to both register_thread_tls and teardown_thread_tls printing tid and
non-null status, then run the failing integration test. Compare: does register see tid X
with valid ptr, and does teardown see the same tid X with a valid ptr? If yes → the walk
executes but the slot state is wrong. If no → tid mismatch between the two calls.

Alternative: maybe the failing path actually goes through
`dispatch_host_thread_create_with_managed_attr` instead of native_pthread_create, because
`lock_and_force_native` sets a thread-local override that doesn't bleed through to
FORCE_NATIVE_THREADING global. Verify by stepping pthread_create and checking which
branch of the `if !force_native && let Some(host_create) = resolved_thread_create_raw()`
guard is taken. If the test is actually using the HOST pthread_create, register_thread_tls
is never called, table_lookup(tid) returns null, write_tls_value falls back to
FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES (thread-local Cell), and teardown_thread_tls early-returns because
table_lookup returns null — destructor never fires. This is consistent with the observed
symptoms: setspecific+getspecific round-trip works (via FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES) but
destructor doesn't fire (because teardown only walks the table path).

That's the most likely explanation. The fix is either:
1. Have write_tls_value to FALLBACK also register the thread in the table so teardown
   finds it, OR
2. Have teardown_thread_tls ALSO walk FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES (it's thread-local so it's
   actually perfectly addressable from inside the exiting thread), OR
3. Fix the test setup to ensure force_native propagates such that pthread_create uses
   native_pthread_create (which DOES call register_thread_tls).

Option 2 is the most robust — thread-exit can always walk FALLBACK because it's
thread-local to the exiting thread, and if any values were stored there, the destructor
should fire."}

guard is taken. If the test is actually using the HOST pthread_create, register_thread_tls
is never called, table_lookup(tid) returns null, write_tls_value falls back to
FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES (thread-local Cell), and teardown_thread_tls early-returns because
table_lookup returns null — destructor never fires. This is consistent with the observed
symptoms: setspecific+getspecific round-trip works (via FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES) but
destructor doesn't fire (because teardown only walks the table path).

That's the most likely explanation. The fix is either:
1. Have write_tls_value to FALLBACK also register the thread in the table so teardown
   finds it, OR
2. Have teardown_thread_tls ALSO walk FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES (it's thread-local so it's
   actually perfectly addressable from inside the exiting thread), OR
3. Fix the test setup to ensure force_native propagates such that pthread_create uses
   native_pthread_create (which DOES call register_thread_tls).

Option 2 is the most robust — thread-exit can always walk FALLBACK because it's
thread-local to the exiting thread, and if any values were stored there, the destructor
should fire.

## FIXED by cc-libc (2026-04-22)

Root cause: pthread_threading_force_native_for_tests() only set the THREAD-LOCAL FORCE_NATIVE_THREADING_OVERRIDE. The tests use `FORCE_NATIVE_ONCE: std::sync::Once` to call it, but `Once::call_once` runs the setter on exactly one thread (whichever cargo-test worker tripped it first). Every OTHER worker thread saw FORCE_NATIVE_THREADING_OVERRIDE = None and fell back to the global atomic FORCE_NATIVE_THREADING, which was never stored true and defaulted to false. So on every worker thread except one, pthread_create routed through the `!force_native` branch into the host libc path (dispatch_host_thread_create_with_managed_attr) — register_thread_tls was never called for the child, teardown_thread_tls never ran at child exit, and the registered TSD destructor was never invoked. The setspecific + getspecific round-trip asserts passed because write_tls_value's fallback path (FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES thread-local Cell) works for the same thread's read.

Fix: set both the global atomic AND the thread-local override in pthread_threading_force_native_for_tests(). Now the first worker to trip FORCE_NATIVE_ONCE flips the global for all subsequent tests/workers.

Also landed a defensive teardown_thread_tls improvement: the walk now falls through to FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES when the TID has no table entry, so any future code path that stores specifics on a non-registered thread still gets its destructor invoked at thread exit (FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES is thread-local so this is safe — only the exiting thread can reach it).

Validation:
- cargo test -p frankenlibc-abi --test pthread_tsd_test -> 14 passed, 0 failed
  (previously 13 passed, 1 failed on tsd_destructor_runs_on_thread_exit).
- cargo test -p frankenlibc-core --lib pthread -> 152 passed, 0 failed.
- cargo test -p frankenlibc-abi --test pthread_abi_test -> 180 passed, 0 failed (regression-tested the pthread surface — no breakage from the global-atomic store).","assignee":"cc-libc","closed_at":"2026-04-22T21:37:35.544419+00:00","close_reason":"Fixed: pthread_threading_force_native_for_tests now sets global atomic, not just thread-local override."}
